claim: Non-vowel structural features (q-initial flag, suffix class, bench-gallows presence, skeleton length, line-position, plain-gallows line-initial) individually add at most +1.9pp to section-prediction accuracy and collectively add only +3.6pp over majority baseline. Combining them with vowel-pattern features slightly DEGRADES performance (38.9% vs 40.1% vowel-only). Per-class precision collapses to only herbal/recipes; the classifier never predicts the other 6 sections.
evidence_for: Vowel-only multi-class accuracy 40.1% on stratified holdout
Single-feature gains: suffix +1.9pp, skel_len +1.5pp, bench +1.3pp, others <=0.6pp
Structural NB predicts only herbal or recipes for all held-out tokens
evidence_against: Q-initial marker is a near-categorical positional feature (98.9%) yet contributes -0.8pp — counterintuitive; may indicate feature is uninformative-for-section rather than uninformative-overall
Single-feature NB may not capture interactions; boosted/tree models could reveal more
test: Non-vowel structural features as primary signal for section prediction
test_threshold: +5pp over majority: useful. <+3pp: uninformative for section task.

tests_run.details: Non-vowel-only classifier 34.1% vs majority 30.5% = +3.6pp gain. Degenerate output distribution (predicts only herbal/recipes).
tests_run.confidence_change: initial 0.85 (high confidence the null is real)

tests_remaining: Try tree/boosted models to capture interactions
Test non-vowel features against non-section targets (image labels, Currier A/B)
reasoning: Published null: structural features beyond vowel patterns do not discriminate section. Q-initial, suffix class, bench-gallows are real glyph-role regularities but not section-informative. Save Brain-V from pursuing structural features for section decoding; redirect toward image-label targets or other task formulations.
